title: Locking Support on Microsoft SQL Server
breadcrumbs: Referential Integrity Management on Microsoft SQL Server
Locking Support on Microsoft SQL Server
text: Locking Support on Microsoft SQL Server Locking Table 1. Locking Support Locking Description Optimistic MSS optimistic locking is used. Cautious MSS update locks are issued when a user starts an update (default). Paranoid MSS update locks are issued when a user starts an update with the read/lock statement. If a deadlock occurs, the MSS connector raises an error in its internal error handling mechanism. When a logon path is marked as deadlocked, no work is allowed on that path until the path transaction is ended. read/lock CAUTION: Using the read /lock statement immediately forces a holdlock in MS-SQL, that prevents all other users from writing to the record concerned (or to any other record in the locked page); this does not prevent users from reading these records, however. If another user tries to write to a record that has a holdlock applied to it, the operation fails and the following occurs: Microsoft SQL Server waits for the blocking lock to be released with a time-out specified by the lock timeout assignment setting. If the lock is not released, a deadlock develops and the transaction fails for the user having the least CPU time in their transaction. Normally, Uniface sets $status to -10 if a user tries to modify and store a record which has been locked by another user, but because Microsoft SQL Server does not return a status in this situation, Uniface is unable to do this. Lock Granularity Under normal circumstances, the unit of locking is left to the DBMS. It is possible to explicitly specify the following locking units: Page lock Row locks Table locks Lock Isolation You can set the isolation of a transaction using the lock isolation connector option. For more information, see lock isolation . Was this topic helpful? Like Dislike
